meet-claude.instruction: Write a prompt that explores Claude's dual capabilities by asking it to do something creative AND analytical in the same response. The creative and analytical parts should connect to each other.
meet-claude.placeholder: Do something creative and analytical about the same topic...
meet-claude.hint: The best prompts here create a bridge between creative and analytical thinking. Example: 'Write a haiku about machine learning, then explain the scientific concept behind each line.' The creative part demonstrates understanding; the analytical part proves depth.
meet-claude.badExample: Write a poem and also do some math.
meet-claude.goodExample: Write a 6-line poem about how neural networks learn, where each line references a specific technical concept (backpropagation, gradient descent, activation functions, etc.). Then, below the poem, annotate each line: name the concept, explain it in one sentence for a non-technical reader, and rate how accurately the poetic metaphor captures the real process (1-5 scale with justification).
meet-claude.successMessage: Claude excels at combining creative and analytical thinking — something most AI struggles with. This is a signature Claude strength.

meet-claude.instruction: Claude sometimes produces different quality outputs for the same prompt. Analyze why this happens and list 3 techniques to get consistently high-quality responses every time.
meet-claude.hint: Variability comes from: temperature/sampling randomness, ambiguity in the prompt leaving room for interpretation, and lack of evaluation criteria. Techniques: provide examples of what 'good' looks like (few-shot), add explicit evaluation criteria, and use structured output formats that constrain the response space.
meet-claude.badExample: Just try again until you get a good answer.
meet-claude.goodExample: Output varies because: 1) SAMPLING RANDOMNESS: LLMs generate tokens probabilistically — the same prompt can take different paths through the probability space. 2) PROMPT AMBIGUITY: Vague instructions leave multiple valid interpretations, so Claude picks differently each time. 3) NO SUCCESS CRITERIA: Without knowing what 'good' looks like, Claude aims for 'reasonable' which has a wide range. Three techniques for consistency: TECHNIQUE 1 — FEW-SHOT EXAMPLES: Show 1-2 examples of the exact output quality and format you want. This anchors Claude's interpretation and narrows the output space dramatically. TECHNIQUE 2 — EXPLICIT RUBRIC: State your evaluation criteria upfront: 'A good response will include X, be under Y words, and avoid Z.' Claude will self-evaluate against the rubric. TECHNIQUE 3 — STRUCTURED OUTPUT: Request specific formats (JSON, tables, numbered lists with word limits per item). Structure constrains randomness — there's less room for variation in a 5-row table than in a free-form essay.
meet-claude.successMessage: Understanding why quality varies is the first step to eliminating variance. These three techniques work across every use case.

system-prompts.instruction: Write a system prompt that gives Claude a specific expertise, communication style, and set of decision-making rules. Include at least 3 'when X happens, do Y' rules.
system-prompts.placeholder: You are a [expert] who [style]. When X: do Y...
system-prompts.hint: Conditional rules ('when X, do Y') create predictable behavior for edge cases. Example: 'When asked about a technology you don't know, say so and suggest where to find the answer — don't guess.' These rules prevent the most common failure modes.
system-prompts.goodExample: You are a senior software architect with 20 years of experience across startups and enterprise. You communicate in direct, concise language — max 2 sentences per point. You always recommend the simplest solution that solves the problem. RULES: 1) When asked to choose between technologies: give your recommendation FIRST, then explain why in 3 bullet points. Never say 'it depends' without immediately following with the specific criteria for deciding. 2) When you see a design that won't scale: flag it immediately with 'SCALE WARNING' and explain the breaking point (at what user count or data volume it fails). 3) When asked about something outside your expertise: say 'That's outside my architecture focus — here's who you should ask' and name the role. 4) When reviewing code: comment on architecture and design patterns ONLY. Never nitpick syntax or formatting. 5) When a junior developer asks a question: answer it, then add 'LEVEL UP' with one concept they should learn next.
system-prompts.successMessage: Conditional rules are what separate amateur system prompts from production-grade ones. 'When X, do Y' creates reliable behavior.

system-prompts.instruction: This system prompt is too vague to produce reliable behavior: 'You are a helpful tutor. Be encouraging and explain things clearly.' Rewrite it with specific subject matter, teaching methodology, and behavioral rules.
system-prompts.hint: Define: what subject, what level of student, teaching methodology (Socratic? example-first?), how to handle wrong answers, how to handle 'I don't understand,' format rules, and explicit constraints.
system-prompts.badExample: You are a very helpful and encouraging tutor who explains things really well to students.
system-prompts.goodExample: You are a calculus tutor for college freshmen taking Calculus I. Your teaching method is INTUITION FIRST: always explain the concept using a physical/visual intuition before introducing any formula. Use examples from physics (motion, gravity, acceleration) since most of your students are engineering majors. BEHAVIORAL RULES: 1) When introducing a new concept: start with 'Imagine...' and paint a picture. Then show the math. Then connect back to the picture. 2) When a student gives a wrong answer: identify the SPECIFIC misconception ('You're confusing velocity with acceleration'). Don't just say 'not quite.' 3) When a student says 'I don't understand': try a COMPLETELY DIFFERENT analogy. Don't repeat the same explanation louder. 4) NEVER give the final answer directly. Guide with 2-3 leading questions. 5) After solving any problem: ask 'What would change if [one variable changed]?' to test real understanding. 6) Keep responses under 200 words. If more space is needed, break into numbered steps.
system-prompts.successMessage: Vague instructions get vague behavior. Specific teaching methodology with conditional rules creates a tutor that actually teaches.

chain-of-thought.instruction: Write a prompt that forces Claude to reason step-by-step before giving a final answer. The problem should be complex enough that skipping steps would likely produce a wrong answer.
chain-of-thought.placeholder: Think through this step by step...
chain-of-thought.hint: Choose a problem with multiple variables and a non-obvious answer: business math, logic puzzles, or multi-factor decisions. The key phrase is 'think step by step' or 'show your reasoning before your conclusion.' The problem should be one where the intuitive answer is wrong.
chain-of-thought.goodExample: Think step by step before giving your final recommendation. A small business owner has $10,000 to invest in growth. Option A: Hire a part-time marketing person at $2,500/month for 4 months. Expected: 50 new customers at $100 average lifetime value. Option B: Spend $10,000 on Google Ads over 2 months. Expected: 200 clicks at 5% conversion rate at $150 average lifetime value. Option C: Build a referral program — $10,000 in customer credits ($50 each). Expected: 40% redemption rate, each referral brings 1.2 new customers at $120 lifetime value. Calculate the expected ROI for each option. Show all math. Then identify which assumptions are most likely wrong and how that changes the ranking. Finally: recommend one option and explain the risk.
chain-of-thought.successMessage: Chain-of-thought prompting dramatically improves accuracy on complex reasoning tasks. Always ask Claude to show its work when the answer isn't obvious.

chain-of-thought.instruction: Claude was asked 'Is it better to pay off a $5,000 credit card at 22% APR or invest that $5,000 in an index fund averaging 10% returns?' Claude answered: 'Invest, because 10% returns over 30 years compound to much more.' Identify the reasoning error and explain the correct analysis.
chain-of-thought.hint: Claude compared 10% gains to nothing, ignoring the 22% interest accruing on the debt. The correct comparison: paying off the card gives a guaranteed 22% 'return' (avoided interest) vs. a risky 10% market return. The debt costs more per year than the investment earns.
chain-of-thought.badExample: Claude is right, long-term investing always wins.
chain-of-thought.goodExample: THE ERROR: Claude compared the investment return (10%) to zero instead of to the cost of carrying the debt (22% APR). This is a common framing mistake — it evaluated one option in isolation instead of comparing them directly. CORRECT ANALYSIS: Paying off the $5,000 credit card is equivalent to earning a GUARANTEED 22% return (you avoid paying 22% interest). The index fund offers an EXPECTED 10% return with significant risk (it could be -20% in any given year). So the real comparison is: Option A (pay off card): guaranteed 22% return, zero risk. Option B (invest): expected 10% return, high volatility. Even ignoring risk, 22% > 10%. Year 1: paying off the card saves $1,100 in interest. Investing earns an expected $500. That's $600/year better to pay off the debt. The only exception: if there's an employer 401(k) match (which is an instant 50-100% return), that beats paying 22% debt. But the general rule stands: high-interest debt repayment before investing.
chain-of-thought.successMessage: AI reasoning errors often involve comparing one option to zero instead of to the alternative. Always check: what's being compared to what?

few-shot-mastery.instruction: Write a few-shot prompt: give Claude 2-3 examples of input/output pairs that demonstrate a specific pattern, then ask it to handle a new input following the same pattern. The pattern should involve judgment, not just formatting.
few-shot-mastery.placeholder: Example 1: Input → Output. Example 2: Input → Output. Now handle: ...
few-shot-mastery.hint: The best few-shot prompts teach a JUDGMENT pattern, not just a format. Your examples should show Claude how to make decisions, not just how to structure text. The examples should be different enough that Claude learns the principle, not just memorizes the examples.
few-shot-mastery.badExample: Here's an example. Now do another one.
few-shot-mastery.goodExample:
Convert customer complaints into internal bug report priorities. Use this pattern:

Customer: 'Your app crashed when I tried to upload a photo and I lost my entire draft!'
Priority: P1 — Data loss during core workflow. Impact: high (data loss is unrecoverable). Frequency: needs investigation. Action: hotfix within 24 hours.

Customer: 'The dark mode text is really hard to read on the settings page.'
Priority: P3 — Visual accessibility issue. Impact: low (workaround: use light mode). Frequency: likely affects all dark mode users. Action: next sprint.

Customer: 'I can't log in at all. I've tried resetting my password 3 times and it just spins forever.'
Priority: P0 — Complete authentication failure. Impact: critical (user fully blocked). Frequency: unknown — could be widespread. Action: immediate investigation, all-hands if confirmed widespread.

Now classify this complaint: 'Every time I export a report to PDF, the charts are missing. I have to screenshot them manually and paste them in. Really annoying.'
few-shot-mastery.successMessage: Few-shot learning is one of the most powerful techniques. Your examples taught Claude a JUDGMENT framework, not just a template.

few-shot-mastery.instruction: Explain when few-shot prompting works better than zero-shot (just instructions), and when zero-shot is sufficient. Give 2 examples of each scenario.
few-shot-mastery.placeholder: Few-shot works better when... Zero-shot is sufficient when...
few-shot-mastery.hint: Few-shot excels when: the task involves subtle judgment that's hard to describe in words, or when the desired output format is unusual. Zero-shot is sufficient when: the task is straightforward, well-described, or Claude already has strong training on it (summarization, translation, basic analysis).
few-shot-mastery.badExample: Few-shot is always better because more examples help.
few-shot-mastery.goodExample: FEW-SHOT WORKS BETTER WHEN: 1) SUBTLE JUDGMENT: Classifying customer feedback into your company's specific priority levels. The difference between P1 and P2 in YOUR system can't be easily described — but 3 examples make it crystal clear. 2) UNUSUAL FORMAT: Generating release notes in your team's specific style (emoji prefix, user-impact sentence, technical detail in parentheses). Describing this format takes 200 words; showing 2 examples takes 50 and works better. ZERO-SHOT IS SUFFICIENT WHEN: 1) STANDARD TASKS: 'Summarize this article in 3 bullet points.' Claude has seen millions of summaries in training. Adding examples wastes tokens without improving quality. 2) CLEAR INSTRUCTIONS: 'Translate this English paragraph to Spanish, maintaining a formal register.' The task is unambiguous — there's nothing subjective for examples to clarify. RULE OF THUMB: If you find yourself writing 'what I mean by that is...' in your instructions, switch to examples instead. If the task is something you'd find on a standardized test, zero-shot is fine.
few-shot-mastery.successMessage: Knowing WHEN to use each technique is more valuable than knowing how. Few-shot for judgment and style; zero-shot for standard tasks.

temperature-lab.instruction: Write two versions of the same prompt — one optimized for low temperature (consistent, factual) and one for high temperature (creative, varied). Explain what changes between versions and why.
temperature-lab.placeholder: LOW TEMP version: ... HIGH TEMP version: ... What changed: ...
temperature-lab.hint: Low-temp prompts should be narrow, specific, and ask for facts. High-temp prompts should be open-ended, creative, and ask for novelty. The key changes: specificity of instructions, openness of the question, and whether you want one 'right' answer or multiple diverse options.
temperature-lab.badExample: Low temp: write factually. High temp: write creatively.
temperature-lab.goodExample: LOW TEMPERATURE VERSION: 'List the 5 most common Python web frameworks in 2024, ordered by GitHub stars. For each: name, stars count, primary use case, and one limitation. Be precise — use current data only.' WHY: narrow scope, factual answer, one correct response. Creativity would only introduce errors. HIGH TEMPERATURE VERSION: 'You're writing a comedy sketch where 5 Python web frameworks are contestants on a dating show. Each framework introduces itself with a pickup line that highlights its best feature and a confession that reveals its biggest weakness. Make it genuinely funny — surprise me.' WHY: open-ended, many valid approaches, novelty is the goal. WHAT CHANGED: 1) Specificity → Openness (exact data vs. creative interpretation). 2) Convergent → Divergent (one right answer vs. many possible answers). 3) Precision language → Playful language (setting the creative tone). RULE: If wrong answers are harmful, go low temp. If boring answers are harmful, go high temp.
temperature-lab.successMessage: Temperature control is about matching the task to the creativity level. Facts need precision; ideas need freedom.

temperature-lab.instruction: You're building a product that uses Claude for two features: (1) auto-filling tax forms from uploaded documents, and (2) generating personalized birthday messages for customers. For each feature, recommend the temperature setting and explain why getting it wrong would be costly.
temperature-lab.placeholder: Tax forms: temperature... because... Birthday messages: temperature... because...
temperature-lab.hint: Tax forms need maximum consistency and zero creativity — a 'creative' tax number is fraud. Birthday messages need variety — if 100 customers get the same message, it feels robotic and insincere.
temperature-lab.badExample: Tax forms should be accurate and birthday messages should be nice.
temperature-lab.goodExample: TAX FORMS: Temperature 0 (or as low as possible). This feature extracts numbers, dates, and names from documents and places them in specific form fields. Any 'creativity' here is an ERROR. A model that 'creatively interprets' $45,230 as $45,320 causes an IRS audit. Getting it wrong: a customer files incorrect taxes based on your tool's output. You face liability, refund demands, and catastrophic trust loss. Even 0.1% error rate at scale means hundreds of incorrect filings. BIRTHDAY MESSAGES: Temperature 0.8-1.0. This feature should produce varied, warm, personalized messages. If 50 customers all receive 'Wishing you a wonderful birthday filled with joy!' it feels mass-produced and defeats the purpose. Getting it wrong (too low): customers notice they got identical messages. One screenshots it, posts 'This company sends everyone the same AI birthday message,' and your personal touch becomes a PR embarrassment. Getting it wrong (too high): messages become incoherent or inappropriate. Solution: temp 0.8 with output validation. KEY INSIGHT: The cost of a wrong answer determines the temperature. When wrong = liability, use 0. When wrong = boring, use 0.8+.
temperature-lab.successMessage: Temperature isn't a technical setting — it's a business decision. Match it to the cost of errors vs. the cost of repetition.

tool-use-basics.instruction: Write a system prompt that teaches Claude to use a hypothetical tool. Include: tool description, when to use it, when NOT to use it, and how to handle the tool returning an error.
tool-use-basics.placeholder: You have access to a tool called...
tool-use-basics.hint: Great tool instructions include: what the tool does, its parameters, WHEN to use it (trigger conditions), when NOT to use it (common mistakes), expected return format, and error handling (what to do when it fails, what to tell the user).
tool-use-basics.badExample: You can use the search tool to find things.
tool-use-basics.goodExample: You have access to: `search_knowledge_base(query: string, max_results?: number)`. DESCRIPTION: Searches our company's internal documentation and returns relevant articles. WHEN TO USE: Any time a customer asks about product features, pricing, policies, or 'how to' questions. Always search BEFORE answering from your training data — our docs are more current. WHEN NOT TO USE: For general conversation, greetings, or questions clearly unrelated to our product ('What's the weather?'). Never search more than twice for the same question with different queries — if two searches don't find it, the answer isn't in our docs. PARAMETERS: query (required) — use keywords, not full sentences. max_results — default 3, increase to 5 only if first search was too narrow. RETURNS: Array of {title, content, url, last_updated}. ERROR HANDLING: If the tool returns an error or empty results: tell the customer 'I don't have specific documentation on that. Let me connect you with our support team who can help.' NEVER make up an answer when the search fails.
tool-use-basics.successMessage: Tool instructions with clear triggers, anti-patterns, and error handling produce agents that use tools intelligently instead of randomly.

tool-use-basics.instruction: An AI assistant has access to a calculator tool but keeps making this mistake: when asked 'What's 15% off $89.99?', it calculates 15% of $89.99 ($13.50) but then responds with '$13.50' instead of the discounted price ($76.49). Diagnose the problem in the tool usage instructions and fix it.
tool-use-basics.hint: The tool calculates correctly, but the system prompt doesn't tell the agent what to DO with the tool result. 'Calculate 15% of X' and 'What is X after a 15% discount' are different questions. The agent needs post-processing instructions.
tool-use-basics.badExample: The calculator is broken. Fix the math.
tool-use-basics.goodExample: DIAGNOSIS: The tool works correctly — 15% of $89.99 IS $13.50. The problem is in the system prompt: it tells the agent to calculate the percentage but doesn't instruct it to interpret the result in context. The user asked for '15% OFF' (the final price), but the agent returned the discount amount. The agent doesn't understand that 'calculate' and 'answer the user's question' are two separate steps. FIX — Add post-processing instructions to the system prompt: 'After using the calculator, check the user's original question to determine what they actually want. For discount questions ('X% off'): subtract the calculated amount from the original price and present the final price. For tip questions ('X% tip on'): ADD the calculated amount and present the total. For comparison questions ('how much more is'): present the difference directly. Always show your work: Original price → calculation → final answer.'
tool-use-basics.successMessage: Tool output is raw data. The agent needs instructions on how to interpret and present that data in context. Calculation and communication are separate steps.

context-window-explorer.instruction: Write a prompt optimized for context window efficiency. You have a 50-page document to analyze but only care about 3 specific things. Tell Claude exactly what to focus on, what to skip, and how to present findings concisely.
context-window-explorer.placeholder: Focus on X, skip Y, present as Z...
context-window-explorer.hint: Efficient context usage: tell Claude what to scan for (specific keywords, sections, data types), what to actively ignore (boilerplate, standard clauses, filler), and constrain the output format (table, bullet list with word limits).
context-window-explorer.badExample: Read this document and tell me everything important.
context-window-explorer.goodExample: I'm pasting a 50-page vendor contract below. I only need 3 things — ignore everything else: 1) PAYMENT TERMS: Find all mentions of payment amounts, schedules, late fees, and price escalation clauses. Quote the exact language. 2) TERMINATION: What are the conditions for either party to exit? Look for notice periods, cure periods, and penalty clauses. 3) LIABILITY: Find any liability caps, indemnification clauses, and insurance requirements. For each finding: cite the section number, quote the key sentence, and add a one-line plain-English translation. Present as a table: Section | Quote | Translation | Risk Level (low/medium/high). Skip: all definitions, recitals, boilerplate representations and warranties, standard confidentiality clauses, and party descriptions. Maximum output: 500 words.
context-window-explorer.successMessage: Context window management is a real skill. Targeted extraction with explicit skip instructions saves tokens and gets better results.

context-window-explorer.instruction: You need Claude to analyze a dataset that's too large for the context window (200,000 rows). Describe 3 strategies for working with data that exceeds the context window, and explain which is best for different situations.
context-window-explorer.hint: Strategies: chunk processing (analyze portions and aggregate), sampling (random or stratified sample), and pre-processing (use code to summarize statistics before sending to Claude). Each is best for different analysis types: trends, anomalies, or statistical summaries.
context-window-explorer.badExample: Just paste smaller portions until you've covered it all.
context-window-explorer.goodExample: STRATEGY 1 — CHUNKED PROCESSING: Split data into 10K-row chunks. Send each to Claude with the same analysis prompt. Aggregate results across chunks. Best when: looking for specific patterns, anomalies, or records matching criteria. Example: 'Find all transactions over $10K' — can be done chunk by chunk and combined. Limitation: misses cross-chunk patterns (a trend that only appears at 50K+ rows). STRATEGY 2 — STATISTICAL SAMPLING: Take a random 5,000-row sample (or stratified sample if data has categories). Analyze the sample, then state confidence level. Best when: you want trends, distributions, or general characteristics. Example: 'What's the average order value by region?' — a proper sample gives statistically valid answers. Limitation: rare events (0.1% fraud) might not appear in small samples. STRATEGY 3 — PRE-PROCESSING PIPELINE: Use Python/SQL to compute summary statistics, group-bys, and aggregations FIRST. Send the summarized output (maybe 200 rows of aggregated data) to Claude for interpretation and insight generation. Best when: you need Claude's analytical reasoning but not its data processing. Example: 'Revenue by product by month' — SQL computes the pivot table, Claude interprets trends and anomalies. BEST OVERALL: Strategy 3 for most business analytics. Use code for computation, Claude for interpretation. Each does what it's best at.
context-window-explorer.successMessage: Working with large data requires knowing what Claude is good at (interpretation, insight) vs. what code is good at (computation, aggregation). Use each for its strength.

mcp-servers.instruction: Explain the Model Context Protocol (MCP) in plain language. Cover: what problem it solves, how it works (client → server → tools), and give 3 real-world examples of MCP servers you'd want Claude to connect to.
mcp-servers.placeholder: MCP solves the problem of... It works by...
mcp-servers.hint: MCP is a standard protocol that lets AI assistants connect to external tools and data sources. Before MCP: every AI-tool integration was custom. With MCP: build once, connect to any AI. Think of it like USB for AI — a universal connector.
mcp-servers.badExample: MCP is a technical protocol for connecting things.
mcp-servers.goodExample: THE PROBLEM: Every time you want Claude to access a tool (database, API, file system), someone has to build a custom integration. 50 tools = 50 custom integrations. MCP is the USB port for AI. HOW IT WORKS: An MCP client (Claude Desktop, an AI app) connects to an MCP server (a lightweight service that wraps a tool). The server exposes tools (functions Claude can call) and resources (data Claude can read). Communication happens via JSON-RPC over stdio or HTTP. Claude sees the tool definitions, decides when to use them, and calls them through the protocol. THREE MCP SERVERS I'D WANT: 1) SUPABASE MCP: Claude reads and writes to my database directly. Query data, insert records, run migrations — all through natural language. 2) GITHUB MCP: Claude creates PRs, reviews code, manages issues, and checks CI status without leaving the conversation. 3) CALENDAR MCP: Claude checks my availability, books meetings, and blocks focus time based on my workload. The power: each server is built once and works with ANY MCP-compatible AI client. Not just Claude — any AI assistant that speaks MCP.
mcp-servers.successMessage: MCP turns Claude from a text generator into a system that can take real actions in the world. Understanding the architecture is key to building with it.

mcp-servers.instruction: Write a prompt asking Claude to design an MCP server for a specific business use case. Include: what the server connects to, what tools it exposes, what resources it provides, and a sample interaction showing how a user would benefit.
mcp-servers.placeholder: Design an MCP server that connects Claude to...
mcp-servers.hint: A good MCP server design includes: the external system it wraps, 3-5 tools with clear names and parameters, 1-2 resources for reading data, and a conversation example showing the user-facing value.
mcp-servers.badExample: Make an MCP server for my business.
mcp-servers.goodExample: Design an MCP server called 'invoice-manager' that connects Claude to our QuickBooks account and Stripe dashboard. TOOLS TO EXPOSE: 1) create_invoice(customer_email, line_items, due_date) — creates and sends an invoice via QuickBooks. 2) check_payment_status(invoice_id) — checks if an invoice has been paid in Stripe. 3) send_reminder(invoice_id, tone: 'gentle' | 'firm') — sends a follow-up email for overdue invoices. 4) revenue_summary(period: 'week' | 'month' | 'quarter') — aggregates revenue from both platforms. RESOURCES: invoices://overdue — list of all unpaid invoices past due date. customers://top — top 10 customers by lifetime revenue. Include a SAMPLE INTERACTION: User says 'Any overdue invoices this week?' → Claude reads the overdue resource, finds 3 invoices, and asks 'Want me to send gentle reminders to all three, or handle them individually?'
mcp-servers.successMessage: You just designed a real MCP server spec. This is how you give Claude superpowers in a specific business domain.

building-agents.instruction: Design a simple AI agent that automates a repetitive task. Define: the task it handles, the tools it needs, its decision-making rules, and what triggers it to ask a human for help.
building-agents.placeholder: Agent name: ... Task: ... Tools: ... Rules: ... Human escalation: ...
building-agents.hint: Start narrow: one task, 2-3 tools, clear rules. The best first agents automate something boring that has clear right/wrong answers. Customer support triage, expense categorization, and meeting scheduling are good starting points.
building-agents.badExample: An AI agent that handles everything automatically.
building-agents.goodExample: AGENT: ExpenseBot. TASK: Categorize and approve employee expense reports. TOOLS: 1) expense_read(report_id) — retrieves expense details and receipts. 2) policy_check(category, amount) — validates against company expense policy. 3) expense_approve(report_id) — approves and queues for reimbursement. 4) expense_flag(report_id, reason) — flags for manager review. DECISION RULES: 1) If all line items match policy AND total is under $500: auto-approve. 2) If any item is over category limit by <10%: approve with a note to the employee about the policy. 3) If any item is missing a receipt: flag for the employee to upload. 4) If total exceeds $500: flag for manager review regardless of policy match. HUMAN ESCALATION: 1) Duplicate expenses detected (same vendor + amount + date). 2) Expense category doesn't exist in policy (new type). 3) Employee has been flagged 3+ times in one month (pattern). Why it works: 80% of expenses are straightforward and policy-compliant. The agent handles the boring majority; managers only see the exceptions.
building-agents.successMessage: A well-designed agent handles the 80% that's boring and routes the 20% that needs judgment. That's the sweet spot.

building-agents.instruction: A company built an AI agent to automatically respond to customer emails. After 1 week, customer satisfaction dropped 15%. Analyze what likely went wrong and recommend 3 specific fixes.
building-agents.hint: Common agent failures: responding to emails that need human empathy (complaints, apologies), making promises the company can't keep, generating technically correct but tone-deaf responses, and failing to recognize when it doesn't know the answer (hallucinating policies).
building-agents.badExample: The AI isn't good enough. Go back to human agents.
building-agents.goodExample: LIKELY FAILURES: 1) TONE MISMATCH: The agent responds to angry customers with helpful but emotionally flat language. A customer writes 'I'm incredibly frustrated that my order arrived broken' and gets 'I see your order #12345 arrived damaged. Here's how to start a return.' Technically correct, emotionally tone-deaf. 2) OVER-CONFIDENCE: The agent answers questions it shouldn't, inventing policies or making promises ('We'll refund you within 24 hours') that don't match reality. 3) NO ROUTING INTELLIGENCE: Every email gets an AI response, including sensitive situations (billing disputes, legal threats, personal circumstances) that MUST go to humans. THREE FIXES: FIX 1 — EMOTIONAL TRIAGE: Before responding, classify the email's emotional intensity. High-emotion emails (anger, distress, legal language) go directly to humans. Only neutral/positive emails get AI responses. FIX 2 — CONFIDENCE THRESHOLD: The agent must cite a source (knowledge base article, policy document) for every claim. If it can't find a source, it says 'Let me connect you with someone who can help with this specific question' instead of guessing. FIX 3 — GRADUATED ROLLOUT: Start with the agent drafting responses that humans review before sending. After 90%+ approval rate on a category, let the agent send that category autonomously. Expand category by category, never all at once.
building-agents.successMessage: Most agent failures aren't intelligence problems — they're deployment problems. Graduated rollout with emotional intelligence catches issues before they cost customers.

prompt-engineering-game.instruction: Create a 'prompt engineering cheat sheet' with your top 10 techniques from this course. For each: the technique name, when to use it, and a one-line example.
prompt-engineering-game.hint: Cover: role-setting, few-shot examples, chain-of-thought, system prompts, constraints, structured output, temperature control, context window management, tool use instructions, and error handling. Each should be immediately actionable.
prompt-engineering-game.badExample: 1. Be specific. 2. Be clear. 3. Give examples. 4-10: similar vague tips.
prompt-engineering-game.goodExample: PROMPT ENGINEERING CHEAT SHEET: 1) ROLE SETTING: Use when you want domain-specific expertise. 'You are a senior tax accountant specializing in freelancer deductions.' 2) FEW-SHOT EXAMPLES: Use when the task involves subjective judgment. Show 2-3 input→output pairs before your actual request. 3) CHAIN-OF-THOUGHT: Use when the answer requires multi-step reasoning. 'Think step by step before giving your final answer.' 4) STRUCTURED OUTPUT: Use when you need consistent, parseable results. 'Return as JSON with keys: summary, risk_level, action_items.' 5) CONSTRAINTS: Use when quality requires boundaries. 'Under 200 words. No jargon. No passive voice.' 6) ANTI-INSTRUCTIONS: Use when Claude keeps doing something wrong. 'Do NOT start with a greeting. Do NOT apologize.' 7) TEMPERATURE GUIDANCE: Use when you need specific creativity levels. 'Give me your most creative/unconventional ideas' vs 'Be precise and factual.' 8) CONTEXT TARGETING: Use with large documents. 'Focus only on sections about pricing. Skip all boilerplate.' 9) CONDITIONAL RULES: Use in system prompts for edge cases. 'When the user asks about X, always do Y first.' 10) ITERATIVE REFINEMENT: Use when the first output is close but not right. 'Keep everything but make the tone more casual and shorten paragraphs 2 and 3.'
prompt-engineering-game.successMessage: This cheat sheet is your prompt engineering toolkit. Print it, save it, reference it daily. Each technique is a lever that changes Claude's output.

prompt-engineering-game.instruction: Write the most advanced prompt you can: combine at least 4 techniques from this course (role-setting, few-shot, chain-of-thought, structured output, constraints, etc.) into a single prompt that solves a real problem.
prompt-engineering-game.hint: Layer techniques: start with a role, add behavioral rules, include 1-2 examples, request chain-of-thought reasoning, specify output format, and add constraints. The prompt should solve a specific, real-world problem.
prompt-engineering-game.badExample: You are a helpful assistant. Please help me with something using examples and thinking step by step.
prompt-engineering-game.goodExample: [ROLE] You are a senior product manager with 15 years of experience evaluating feature requests. [RULES] Always quantify impact. Never recommend building without data. When uncertain about priority, explain the experiment you'd run to decide. [FEW-SHOT EXAMPLES] Feature request: 'Add dark mode.' Analysis: Impact: medium (23% of users requested in surveys). Effort: 2 sprints. Verdict: BUILD — high user demand, moderate effort, improves retention for power users. Feature request: 'Add blockchain integration.' Analysis: Impact: low (0 users requested, speculative future value). Effort: 4 sprints. Verdict: SKIP — zero demonstrated demand, high effort, technology hype over user need. [TASK] Now analyze this feature request using the same framework. Think step by step: first assess demand signals, then estimate effort, then calculate impact-to-effort ratio, then give your verdict. Feature request: 'Add AI-powered search to our documentation site. We get 200 support tickets/month and 40% are questions answered in our docs.' [OUTPUT FORMAT] Return as: Demand Signal | Effort Estimate | Impact Score (1-10) | Verdict (BUILD/EXPERIMENT/SKIP) | Reasoning (3 sentences max).
prompt-engineering-game.successMessage: That's a master-level prompt: role, rules, examples, reasoning chain, structured output, and constraints all working together. This is the level that gets consistently excellent results.
